# Questions {.unnumbered}

## Assignment 1: Two-Sample Tests, Permutation Tests & ANOVA

*Source: [Assignment 1](https://web.stanford.edu/class/stats191/assignments/assignment1.html)*

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Dataset: College (ISLR2 package)"}
The `College` dataset from the `ISLR2` package contains various statistics for a large number of US colleges from the 1995 issue of US News and World Report.

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| Private | Factor: Yes/No indicator of private college |
| Room.Board | Room and board costs |
| Top10perc | Percent of new students from top 10% of high school class |
| (many others) | See `?College` for full list |
:::

### Question 1

Install the package `ISLR2` using `install.packages("ISLR2")`. We'll use the `College` data set for this problem. In particular, we want to compare the cost of room and board between private and public colleges.

**(a)** Draw a boxplot of `Room.Board` stratified by `Private`. Similarly, plot histograms of the two samples.

**(b)** Based on the histograms, do you think the two-sample t-test is justified here?

**(c)** Carry out the two-sample t-test comparing `Room.Board` between private and public colleges.

**(d)** Suppose we wished to test whether the average `Room.Board` at private colleges is greater than at public colleges. Carry out this one-sided test.

### Question 2

In this problem, we'll repeat the analysis above using `Top10perc` rather than `Room.Board`. We'll also simulate the distribution of the t-statistic for comparing `Top10perc` between public and private colleges under the null hypothesis that there is no difference.

**(a)** Repeat the steps above swapping `Top10perc` for `Room.Board`. Do the histograms have a normal shape?

**(b)** Compute the two-sample t-statistic comparing `Top10perc` among private and public colleges by saving the `$statistic` attribute of the object returned by `t.test`.

**(c)** We'll create a sample of 10,000 draws of the t-statistic based on random permutations of the data. Randomly shuffle the `Private` vector 10,000 times, and recompute the two-sample t-test statistic, storing the values in a vector of length 10,000.

**(d)** Create a histogram of the 10,000 statistics. Does its shape seem similar to that of the t-distribution used to compute the p-value?

**(e)** Compute a p-value by computing the proportion of your 10,000 statistics that are larger in absolute value than your observed t-statistic. Is it close to the p-value you found by using `t.test`?

### Question 3: Sign Test

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Dataset: Fund (ISLR2 package)"}
The `Fund` dataset from `ISLR2` contains (simulated) returns for different fund managers over 50 months. If fund managers are unable to really beat the market, we expect that they will have positive returns as frequently as negative returns. This is our null hypothesis.
:::

**(a)** For `Manager14`, compute the signs of their 50 returns and store the number of months with positive returns as our statistic.

**(b)** For 10,000 reps, draw 50 standard normals (mean 0, variance 1) using `rnorm(50)` and compute the number of positive entries as a test statistic. Plot a histogram of your results using the argument `breaks=1:50`.

**(c)** Instead of normal random variables, repeat (b) with uniform random variables which have a distribution symmetric around 0.5. That is, for 10,000 reps, simulate 50 uniform random variables with `runif(50)` and store the number greater than 0.5 as a test statistic. Does it look the same as (b)? Why? Explain why the distribution from (b) is a good way to evaluate evidence against our null hypothesis about Manager14.

**(d)** Compute a p-value by computing the proportion of your 10,000 draws that have more positive draws than your observed number of positive months. Is this a 1-sided or 2-sided p-value? What is the null hypothesis here?

**(e)** The distribution of the number of positive standard normals out of 50 is the Binomial distribution with parameters (50, 1/2). Its true histogram (i.e., its probability mass function, or pmf) can be computed with `dbinom`. Use `barplot` to make a barplot of the true histogram.

**(f)** Compute the probability of observing at least as many positive standard normals as the number of positive return months for Manager14. You can do this by summing `dbinom` or using the cumulative distribution function `pbinom`.

### Question 4

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Dataset: Wage (ISLR2 package)"}
The `Wage` dataset from `ISLR2` contains wage and other data for a group of 3000 male workers in the Mid-Atlantic region.

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| wage | Worker's wage |
| logwage | Log of wage |
| maritl | Marital status (5 levels) |
:::

**(a)** Make a boxplot of `wage ~ maritl` (marital status). Inspect histograms for the 5 different values of `maritl`. Do the histograms have a normal shape?

**(b)** Repeat (a) for `logwage`.

**(c)** Fit the one-way ANOVA model `logwage ~ maritl`. Test the null hypothesis that there is no difference in wages based on the marital status of the 3000 men.

**(d)** Construct a 95% confidence interval for the difference between the wage of widowed men and those never married.

**(e)** Suppose you wanted to not rely on the p-value from the F test as provided by your one-way model in (c). How might you simulate a distribution to test the null hypothesis that the wage distribution doesn't depend on marital status?

**(f)** Compute a p-value by simulation using your answer to (e).
